coredata.dc:title: Core-shell Ag@nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots modified BiVO4 nanosheets with enhanced photocatalytic performance under Vis-NIR light: Synergism of molecular oxygen activation and surface plasmon resonance

coredata.dc:description: Visible-near infrared (Vis-NIR) light accounts for ~95% of solar light. In recent years, rational design of Vis-NIR responsive photocatalyst with improved photocatalytic performance has attracted growing concern. In this work, a Vis-NIR responsive photocatalyst was developed by integrating core–shell structured Ag@nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with BiVO4 nanosheets. The efficiencies of tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) removal based on the BiVO4/Ag@N-CQDs composite were 80.37% and 65.67% under visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm) for 60 min and NIR light irradiation (λ > 760 nm) for 180 min, respectively. This improved photocatalytic performance of BiVO4/Ag@N-CQDs composite stems from the synergistic effect between BiVO4 nanosheets and core–shell structured Ag@N-CQDs nanoparticles. The as-prepared BiVO4/Ag@N-CQDs composite exhibits surface plasmon resonance (SPR) and molecular oxygen activation. Thus, the charge separation, light absorption in Vis-NIR region and oxidation ability are boosted during the photocatalytic activity. This work provides a new insight for designing advanced structure of Vis-NIR responsive photocatalyst.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Visible-near infrared (Vis-NIR) light accounts for ~95% of solar light. In recent years, rational design of Vis-NIR responsive photocatalyst with improved photocatalytic performance has attracted growing concern. In this work, a Vis-NIR responsive photocatalyst was developed by integrating core–shell structured Ag@nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with BiVO4 nanosheets. The efficiencies of tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) removal based on the BiVO4/Ag@N-CQDs composite were 80.37% and 65.67% under visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm) for 60 min and NIR light irradiation (λ > 760 nm) for 180 min, respectively. This improved photocatalytic performance of BiVO4/Ag@N-CQDs composite stems from the synergistic effect between BiVO4 nanosheets and core–shell structured Ag@N-CQDs nanoparticles. The as-prepared BiVO4/Ag@N-CQDs composite exhibits surface plasmon resonance (SPR) and molecular oxygen activation. Thus, the charge separation, light absorption in Vis-NIR region and oxidation ability are boosted during the photocatalytic activity. This work provides a new insight for designing advanced structure of Vis-NIR responsive photocatalyst.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the antibiotics have been widely used to prevent bacteria infections. Tetracycline, a typical antibiotic, in water exerts toxicity on non-target organisms and causes antibiotic resistance [1,2]. Thus residual antibiotics in water may pose a threat to ecologicalenvironment and human health [3–5]. Semiconductor-based photocatalysis is regarded as a green technology to degrade antibiotics in water [6,7]. A large number of researches on semiconductors have made certain breakthroughs, such as TiO2, C3N4, BiVO4, Bi2WO6 and CdS [8–11]. Among them, the BiVO4 is considered as a promising candidate for photocatalysis due to its intrinsic advantages, such as relative narrow band gap (2.4–2.5 eV), stability and nontoxicity [12–15].

Although the BiVO4 with unique properties promises a prospect in photocatalysis, the insufficient light utilization and severe charge-carrier recombination extremely restrict its photocatalytic activity [16]. The visible (Vis) and near-infrared (NIR) light account for approximately 95% of solar energy [17,18]. Unfortunately, BiVO4 is hardly to capture and utilize long wavelength Vis-NIR photons with low photonic energy due to its absorption band at about 520 nm [19]. Additionally, the relatively positive conduction band (CB) edge and inevitably recombination of photo-excited electron-hole pairs lead to a reduced redox ability, which is unfavourable for photocatalytic activity [20]. Thus, it’s extremely urgent to develop BiVO4 with long-wavelength Vis-NIR light absorption, high efficiency of charge separation and strong oxidation ability.

The plasmonic metal (e.g., Au and Ag) with surface plasmon resonance (SPR) is introduced into the photocatalytic system as the plasmonic metals simultaneously improve light absorption and charge separation [21,22]. Additionally, plasmonic metals may convert the photons into heat (photothermal effect), which act as an auxiliary approach to accelerate the separation of photo-excited charges. In general, the plasmonic metal can be anchored on the surface of semiconductors to form heterojunction. It will inevitably lead to corrosion and dissolution of plasmonic metals as the exposure of metals to the reactants and surrounding medium [23,24]. It’s expecting that coating conductive materials on the surface of plasmonic metals to protect the metals from photo-corrsion. Additionally, the SPR intensity and wavelength are determined by the shape and size of plasmonic metal [25]. The metal nanoparticles possess photo-absorption ability in the region of 400–600 nm [26,27], which is extraordinary limited for the full utilization of solar energy. Thus, construction of advanced core–shell structures may solve the remained problems.

Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a class of carbon nanomaterial with size ≤ 10 nm, have been widely used in the field of absorption, photovoltaics and photocatalysis [28,29]. CQDs exhibit various advantages, such as chemical stability, up-converted photoluminescence (PL) behavior and unique charge transfer properties [30–32]. However, the application of CQDs is still restricted as the low photosensitization efficiency. The nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) possess sufficient light absorption and effective charge transfer, which is desirable to be introduced into the photocatalytic system [33]. Furthermore, considerable oxygen defects on the surface of N-CQDs can absorb dissolved O2 and then convert them into O2 − radical, which is essential for the photocatalytic activity [33,34].

Herein, the novel Ag@N-CQDs core–shell structures decorated BiVO4 nanosheet was synthesized via a wet impregnation method. The elaborate design of BiVO4/Ag@N-CQDs photocatalyst was based on the following reasons. Firstly, the N-CQDs can act as an electron donor, which may reduce the Ag+ to Ag nanoparticle and form an advanced structure. Secondly, the Ag/N-CQDs composite loaded on the surface of BiVO4 can act not only an electron mediator to facilitate charges transfer, but also a photosensitizer derived from the strong affinity between Ag and N-CQDs. Hence, the as-prepared BiVO4/Ag@N-CQDs samples realized efficient Vis-NIR light absorption, enhanced charge transfer efficiency and improved molecular oxygen activation, which can lead to an improved photocatalytic activity. The photocatalytic performance was investigated by degradation of tetracycline hydrochloride (TC).

Bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), ammonium metavanadate (NH4VO3), silver nitrate (≥99.8%), ethylenediamine (C2H8N2, ≥99%) and ammonium citrate were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). All reagents were used as received from commercial suppliers without further purification. Solutions were freshly prepared with deionized water (18.25 MΩ cm specific resistance).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of BiVO4 nanosheets, N-CQDs and Ag@N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation methods of BiVO4 nanosheets and N-CQDs were based on the previous reports [35,36]. The Ag@N-CQDs composite was prepared by a water bath method. In detail, 500 mg of AgNO3 was dispersed in 50 ml of N-CQDs. Then the resulting solution was immediately transferred to a water bath kettle and reacted at 55 °C for five minutes. Finally, the resulted product was collected, washed with deionized water thoroughly and dried at 40 °C in the vacuum oven.

The BiVO4/Ag@N-CQDs composites were prepared by a simple wet impregnation procedure as follows: The as-prepared BiVO4 nanosheets (100 mg) were dispersed in 50 ml methanol and then sonicated for 30 min. Subsequently, a certain amount of Ag@N-CQDs nanoparticles were added into the above suspension and sonicated for another 30 min. Afterwards, the suspensions were stirred in the fume hood until the solution evaporated completely. Finally, the resulting product was collected and dried at 40 °C in the vacuum oven for 8 h. The mass ratio of Ag@N-CQDs to BiVO4 was controlled at 1%, 2%, 3% and 5%. The resulted BiVO4/Ag@N-CQDs composites were denoted as BiVO4/Ag@NCDs-1, BiVO4/Ag@NCDs-2, BiVO4/Ag@NCDs-3, and BiVO4/Ag@NCDs-5, respectively.

The structural and chemical information of the photocatalysts were measured by an X-ray diffraction (XRD, Cu-Ka, Bruker D8 Advance). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded on a Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer. The field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) was performed on a FEI-quanta 200F scanning electron microscope. Transmission electron micrograph (TEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) were recorded on a JEOL JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope. The UV–Vis-NIR diffuse reflectance (DRS) were detected on a Hitachi UH4150 spectrophotometer. The photocurrent and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) experiments were performed by using a conventional three electrode system on a CHI 660D electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Ltd., China). The electrolyte was 0.1 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured by a fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh FLsp 920). The electron spin resonance (ESR) signals of radicals spin-trapped were performed on a spectrometer (Bruker model ESR E500) under visible (λ > 420 nm) and NIR (λ > 760 nm) light irradiation using DMPO (Sigma Chemical Co.) as spin-trap reagent.

The photocatalytic activity of the samples was evaluated by degradation of tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) and ciprofloxacin (CIP) under Vis and NIR light irradiation. Briefly, 30 mg and 100 mg samples were added into TC (10 mg/L, 100 ml) and CIP (10 mg/L, 100 ml) solution, respectively. Then the mixtures stirred for 30 min under dark condition to achieve adsorption–desorption equilibrium. Afterwards, the photocatalytic reactions were performed under light irradiation. A 300 W Xe lamp (100 mW/cm2, Beijing China Education Au-light, Co., Ltd.) with a 420 nm cutoff filter and a UVVISCUT filter (λ > 760 nm) served as the Vis and NIR light source, respectively. A series of mixed solution were collected in a certain time interval and filtrated by 0.22 μm PTFE syringefilters. The concentrations of TC were monitored by a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600, SHIMADZU Corporation, Japan).

The degradation intermediates of TC were analyzed by a LC-MS/MS (1290/6470 Triple Quad, Agilent) system. Elution was conducted at a flow rate of 0.2 ml/min with H2O containing 0.1% (v/v) formic acid as eluent A and acetonitrile as eluent B. The injected volume was 5 μL and the column temperature was 30 °C. The MS measurement was performed as follows: the capillary voltage was 4.0 kV; the desolvation gas was N2 with flow rate of 10 L/min and the gas temperature was 300 °C; nebulization pressure was 15 psi. The mass range of MS was scanned was 100–600 m/z.

The chemical states and elemental chemical compositions of BiVO4/Ag@N-CQDs were evaluated by XPS. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the survey XPS spectrum indicates that BiVO4/Ag@N-CQDs composite consists of C, N, Ag, Bi, V, O elements and no impurities are detectable. As displayed in Fig. 1b, the main peaks of C 1s can be fitted with four peaks at 284.7, 285.6, 286.6 and 288.2 eV which are assigned to graphitic or aliphatic (C = C and C–C) [36,37], C–N [38], CO [38,39] and OC–O–Ag, respectively. The high resolution of N 1 s XPS spectrum can be divided into three peaks at 399.0, 399.8 and 400.5 eV (Fig. 1c). These peaks corresponding to pyridinic nitrogen, pyrrolic nitrogen and graphitic nitrogen groups, respectively [33,34,40]. As for Ag 3d spectra in Fig. 1d, two characteristic peaks at 367.9 and 373.8 eV belong to the Ag+ 3d5/2 and 3d3/2, respectively [41,42]. Besides, two weak peaks located at 368.3 and 374.2 eV can be assigned to metallic Ag. The XPS spectra of Bi 4f are shown in Fig. 1e. Two characteristic peaks at 159.0 and 164.3 eV can be assigned to Bi3+ in the composite. The V 2p peaks at 516.5 and 524 eV corresponding to V2 p3/2 and V2p1/2 (Fig. 1f), which are the characteristic peaks of V5+ in the composite. The O 1 s XPS spectrum can be divided into three peaks at 529.4, 530 0.0 and 531.7 eV (Fig. 1g), corresponding to Bi-O, O–H and OC–O–Ag, respectively [4,20,38].

The crystalline phases of BiVO4 nanosheets and BiVO4/Ag@N-CQDs composites were analyzed by XRD analyses. As presented in Fig. 2 a, the XRD pattern of BiVO4 nanosheets are agreement with the monoclinic scheelite BiVO4 phase (JCPDS No. 14-0688). Furthermore, the 004 peaks located at 30.57° indicating that the exposed facets of BiVO4 were {001}. The XRD patterns of BiVO4/Ag@N-CQDs composites are similar as that of BiVO4 nanosheets, indicating that the peaks of BiVO4 in XRD spectra are the dominant peaks. The possible reason is the low dosage of Ag@N-CQDs in the composites and the high crystallinity of BiVO4 phase. Notably, a small peak at 38.1° can be detected, which belongs to the characteristic peak of Ag [43]. However, no typical diffraction peaks of N-CQDs can be observed due to its quite low contents in the composites.

The XRD pattern of Ag@N-CQDs is shown in Fig. 2b. The diffraction peaks located at 38.1, 44.3, 64.4, 77.5 and 81.5° can be clearly observed, which refer to (111), (200), (220), (311) and (222) characteristic crystal plane of Ag, respectively. Notably, a new peak at 28.4°, ascribing to the OC–O–Ag, was detected.

The Raman spectra were recorded to confirm the composition of the samples (Fig. 2c). The Raman peaks of BiVO4 can be located at 212.0, 326.6, 369.3, 717.8 and 828.6 cm−1, which confirms that the BiVO4 is monoclinic phase. The Raman spectrum of BiVO4/Ag@N-CQDs composite is similar with that of BiVO4. While two characteristic peaks at 1399.3 and 1601.4 cm−1 can be detected, which are ascribed to the D-band and G-band of N-CQDs, respectively. Furthermore, the N-CQDs spectrum upon de-convolution exhibits two peaks at 1399.3 and 1444.3 cm−1.

The textural characteristics data of the samples were shown in Table 1 . The BiVO4/Ag@NCDs composite possessed higher BET surface area, pore diameter and volume than that of BiVO4 nanosheets. The results indicated that the Ag@Ag@N-CQDs nanoparticles modified on the surface of BiVO4 can increase the BET surface area, pore diameter and volume. This may provide abundant active sites and promote light absorption in the photocatalytic process.

The morphology of the samples was investigated by SEM and TEM. The SEM image reveals that pure BiVO4 exhibited a nanosheet structure and stacked with each other (Fig. 3 a). The SEM image of BiVO4/Ag@N-CQDs composite is shown in Fig. 3-b. The nanoparticles deposited on the surface of BiVO4 nanosheets can be inferred to Ag/N-CQDs composite. The morphology analysis was further performed by TEM characterization, revealing that the BiVO4 nanosheets possessed smooth surfaces (Fig. 3e). While the Ag/N-CQDs composite exhibits a core–shell structure (Fig. 3c-d). The Ag, N and C elemental mapping distributions are presented in Fig. 3j-l, which further confirms that the Ag nanoparticles are wrapped with N-CQDs shells. Additionally, Ag@N-CQDs nanoparticles exhibit an average size of about 87.5 nm. As for BiVO4/Ag@N-CQDs composite (Fig. 3f), the smooth surfaces of the nanosheets become rough, revealing that the core–shell Ag@N-CQDs nanoparticles are deposited on BiVO4 nanosheets. The SEAD image indicates that the composite is polycrystalline structure.

As presented in Fig. 4 a, the N-CQDs exhibit an absorption peak at around 340 nm, corresponding to the n-π* transitions of C = O bands [44]. As for Ag@N-CQDs core–shell structures, the absorption peak moves to 454 nm, which originates from the characteristic surface plasmonic absorption of Ag [45]. This result indicates that the formation of Ag@N-CQDs core–shell structures. Generally, the Ag nanoparticles possess photo-absorption ability in the range of 300–550 nm [27]. While the Ag@N-CQDs core–shell structures exhibit an extended light absorption region in 320–800 nm, indicating the synergetic effect between Ag and N-CQDs in the core–shell structures.

The up-conversion photoluminescence spectra of N-CQDs under different excitation wavelength are shown in Fig. 4b. The results refer that N-CQDs can absorb long wavelength Vis-NIR light (700–900 nm). The up-converted emissions are located at the range of 400–600 nm [8,46], which can be further utilized by Ag and then enhance the SPR effect of Ag. Hence, the Ag@N-CQDs core–shell structures act as an energy transfer component in BiVO4/Ag@N-CQDs composite.

The UV–Vis-NIR diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) of the BiVO4 nanosheets and BiVO4/Ag@N-CQDs composites are presented in Fig. 4c. The BiVO4 nanosheets exhibit the optical absorption edge at ~520 nm, which endows a strong light absorption in UV and visible regions. Noteworthily, BiVO4/Ag@N-CQDs composites exhibit broaden light absorption in visible-NIR light region compared to the pure BiVO4 nanosheets. The light absorption intensity of BiVO4/Ag@N-CQDs composite increases, especially in the region of 550–2500 nm, with the increase of Ag@N-CQDs content in the composites. The slightly red shift of adsorption edges was occurred in the BiVO4/Ag@N-CQDs composites, indicating that the band gaps of the BiVO4/Ag@N-CQDs composites were slightly narrowed. The band gaps (Eg ) of the samples can be calculated by the plot of (αhυ)2 versus optical energy (hυ) [35]. As shown in Fig. 4d, the Eg values of BiVO4 and BiVO4/Ag@NCDs-2 are 2.47 and 2.37 eV, respectively. Furthermore, the band structures of BiVO4 and BiVO4/Ag@NCDs-2 composite were measured by valence band (VB) XPS. The EVB values of BiVO4 and BiVO4/Ag@NCDs-2 composite were estimated to be 2.81 and 2.74 eV, respectively (Fig. 4e). The ECB of samples can be calculated by the formula: ECB = EVB − Eg (1). In this equation, Eg is the band-gap of the sample, and ECB and EVB are the value of CB and VB, respectively. Hence, the ECB values of BiVO4 and BiVO4/Ag@NCDs-2 are 0.34 and 0.35 eV, respectively. The band-gap structure of BiVO4/Ag@NCDs-2 composite is shown in Fig. 4f. Thus, the VB edge and CB edge were only slightly affected by the modification of Ag@N-CQDs.

As presented in Fig. 5 a, the photocurrent density of BiVO4/Ag@NCDs-2 composite is much higher than that of BiVO4 nanosheets. Hence, after the deposition of Ag@N-CQDs, the BiVO4/Ag@NCDs-2 composite exhibits efficient charge separation efficiency. Generally, semicircle diameter of the EIS Nyquist plot means the resistance of the sample [28]. As shown in Fig. 5b, the semicircle diameter of EIS Nyquist plot of BiVO4 nanosheets is larger than that of BiVO4/Ag@NCDs-2 composite. This result demonstrates that the core–shell structured Ag@N-CQDs nanoparticles can improve the photo-excited electron-hole pair separation efficiency and lead to an efficient photocatalytic efficiency.

The charge transfer and separation efficiency of the samples were further investigated by PL spectra. All the photocatalysts exhibit the emission bands located at 530–550 nm under the 540 nm excitation (Fig. 5c). Furthermore, the PL intensity of BiVO4/Ag@NCDs-2 composite obviously decreased when compared with that of the pure BiVO4, meaning that the recombination of photo-excited electron-hole pairs is markedly suppressed. The improved charge transfer efficiency of BiVO4/Ag@NCDs-2 composite should be attributed to the excellent electron capture ability of Ag@N-CQDs in the composite.

The photocatalytic performance of BiVO4 and BiVO4/Ag@NCDs composites were evaluated by photodegradation of TC under visible and NIR light irradiation. As presented in Fig. 6 a, after the visible light irradiation for 60 min, the photodegradation efficiency of pure BiVO4 can reach only 57.96%. After the modification of Ag@N-CQDs on the surface of BiVO4, the photocatalytic activities of BiVO4/Ag@NCDs composites are remarkably improved in the visible light condition. The photodegradation efficiency of BiVO4/Ag@NCDs-2 composite can reach 80.37%. Under NIR light irradiation for 180 min, the pure BiVO4 exhibits no degradation efficiency on TC as its relative large band-gap cannot respond to NIR light (Fig. 6b). Notably, the BiVO4/Ag@NCDs-2 composites exhibit a highest photodegradation efficiency of 65.67% after 180 min under NIR light irradiation. However, the high content of Ag@N-CQDs suppress the photocatalytic performance of the BiVO4/Ag@NCDs composites, which can be ascribed to the strong photon scattering and high charge recombination. The result indicates that the existence of synergistic effect between Ag and N-CQDs.

Considering the possibility of practical application, photocatalytic performance of the photocatalysts were further investigated by degradation of CIP (Fig. 6c and d). For the BiVO4, 33.10% of CIP was degraded under visible light while nearly no degradation under NIR light. As expected, the BiVO4/Ag@NCDs-2 composites exhibit the photodegradation efficiency of 46.36% and 9.70% under visible and NIR light, respectively. Thus, the BiVO4/Ag@NCDs-2 composites possess potential in waste water treatment.

Furthermore, some BiVO4 based photocatalysts for degradation of TC were reported by previous literature. As shown in Table 2 , the optimal TC removal of BiVO4/Ag@N-CQDs was 80.37% and 65.67% under visible and NIR light, respectively. When compared with other BiVO4 based photocatalysts, BiVO4/Ag@N-CQDs possess preponderant photocatalytic performance, especially under NIR region.

To investigate the pH affect of the photocatalytic reactions, the TC photodegradation with different initial pH values were performed. The pH values of the TC solutions were 3, 5, 7, 8, 9 and 11 which were adjusted by 0.01 M NaOH and 0.01 M HNO3. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the pH values of the solution posed a great affect on the photocdegradation of TC. Notably, the photodegradation efficiency reached 85.8% when the pH was adjusted to 7. However, the solution of pH = 3 and 11 posed a negative effect on photocatalytic activity. Briefly, the weak weak acid/alkali and neutral conditions conditions are beneficial for the photodegradation of TC.

The recycle experiments for photodegradation of TC were performed under visible light to investigate the photocatalytic stability of BiVO4/Ag@N-CQDs composite. As shown in Fig. 8 a, after four recycle experiments, the degradation efficiency of TC still remains at 76.1%, indicating that the BiVO4/Ag@NCDs-2 composite exhibits good stability in the photocatalytic reaction process. Furthermore, the XPS measurements of the BiVO4/Ag@NCDs-2 composite after recycle experiment were conducted. As illustrated in Fig. 8b-d, there is no obvious changes can be found in the survey and high-resolution XPS spectra of the BiVO4/Ag@NCDs-2 composite after the photocatalytic activities. The result proves that the excellent stability of BiVO4/Ag@NCDs composites. As for the high-resolution XPS spectra of Ag 3d, the peaks at 368.3 and 374.2 eV increased after photocatalytic reaction process, indicating that the part of Ag species were transformed to OC–O–Ag.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism for the photodegradation of TC
ce:section.ce:para: Free radical trapping experiments of superoxide radical (•O2 −), hydroxyl radical (•OH) and hole (h+) were conducted under visible and NIR light to investigate the photocatalytic mechanism. In this study, purging N2, 4-hydroxy-2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine-N-oxyl (TEMPOL), triethanolamine (TEA) and isopropanol (IPA) were the scavenger for O2 −, h+ and •OH in the photocatalytic reaction process, respectively [4,62,63]. As shown in Fig. 9 a, the photodegradation efficiency of TC was obviously inhibited when the existence of N2, TEMPOL and TEA. The photodegradation efficiency of TC declined from 80.70% to 63.86%, 65.95% and 70.13% under visible light, respectively. Furthermore, the addition of IPA scavenger had slight effect on the photocatalytic reaction. Similarly, the photodegradation efficiency of TC declined from 65.80% to 46.89%, 50.30% and 59.82% when the existence of N2, TEMPOL and TEA under NIR light, respectively. The addition of IPA posed a slight effect on the TC degradation (Fig. 9b). Consequently, the •O2 − , h+ and •OH contribute to the photodegradation of TC and the •O2 − plays the dominate role during this process.

To further investigate the mechanism of photocatalytic process under visible and NIR irradiation, the ESR measurements were performed using DMPO as free radical scavenger [64]. As shown in Fig. 9c, no signal can be found under the dark condition and the main characteristic peaks of DMPO-O2 − can be detected under visible light irradiation. Additionally, the quartet peaks (intensity ratio 1:2:2:1) of DMPO- •OH were obtained under the same light condition (Fig. 9d). Similarly, under the NIR irradiation, the singles of DMPO-O2 − and DMPO-OH both can be detected. Thus, both the superoxide radical and hydroxyl radical participated in the photocatalytic reaction under Vis/NIR condition (Fig. 9e–f). These results show that the introduction of Ag@N-CQDs can promote the production of reactive oxygen species.

The oxygen vacancies (OVs) can play as an active and charge separation site, which is essential for the photocatalytic activity. Most importantly, the •O2 − are produced via single electron reduction at OVs because the fact that the dissolved oxygen in solution trends to be charged by the local electrons of OVs [65]. To investigate the OVs of the as-prepared samples, the low-temperature electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) analysis was conducted (Fig. 10 ). As for the pure BiVO4, a weak signal is observed at g = 2.003, which can be ascribed to the OVs on the {001} facets of BiVO4 [66]. Notably, the strong characteristic signal at g = 2.003 of BiVO4/Ag@NCDs-2 composite is observed, which can be deduced to the modification of Ag@N-CQDs on the surface of BiVO4. As a large population of diverse defects on N-CQDs can directly contact with surrounding mediator, thus the dissolved molecular oxygen can be activated by N-CQDs [34].

The photothermal effect refers to the conversion of photon energy to thermal energy, which is regarded as an auxiliary way to promote the photocatalytic activity. The photothermal materials, such as metal and carbon materials, act as a heat generator at the reaction interface [67,68]. Then the heat of materials dissipate to the surrounding solution. Generally, increasing reaction temperature can reduce the apparent activation energy of the photocatalytic system, resulting in an improved photocatalytic performance [27]. To study the photothermal effect of the samples, surface temperature of the BiVO4 nanosheets and BiVO4/Ag@NCDs-2 composite were recorded by a thermal imaging device. All the as-prepared samples were exposed to a LED lamp for 10, 20, 30 min, the thermo images were taken. As shown in Fig. 11 , the surface temperature of BiVO4 nanosheets and BiVO4/Ag@NCDs-2 composite increased with illumination time. Notably, the surface temperature of BiVO4/Ag@NCDs-2 composite was greatly increased to 82.3 °C after 60 min illumination. While the surface temperature of BiVO4 nanosheets increased gradually from 25.4 °C to 57.9 °C. Generally, the total thermal energy stems from two parts. The one is the light source, and the other one is photothermal effect. Thermal changes caused by external light source are invariable. Thus, the increment of thermal energy of the BiVO4 nanosheets can be ascribed to external light sources. Most importantly, photothermal effect takes place in the BiVO4/Ag@NCDs-2 composite. The possible reason is that the synergy of N-CQDs and SPR effect of Ag, which promotes the photothermal effect.

To investigate the reaction process specifically, the degradation intermediates at different reaction time were detected by LC-MS technique (Fig. 12 a–c). On the basis of degradation intermediates identification and reactive sites prediction, the possible degradation pathways of TC were proposed (Fig. 12d). The dominant peak at 445.1 can be ascribed to the original TC (Fig. 12a). Generally, the N-methyl, amino group, ketone group, aromatic ring and double bond are reactive sites [69,70]. The degradation pathways of TC mainly including two parts: the cleavage of specific functional groups and the ring-opening reactions. As illustrated in Path I, the TC (m/z = 445.1) can transform to TC 1 (m/z = 461.1) initially via a hydroxylation process due to the fact that the OH tends to attack unsaturated carbon bonds (CC) [71]. Afterwards, the N-demethylation is prone to be achieved as the C-N is too weak to the attacking of radicals [72]. The addition of electron at the ketone position of TC 2 (m/z = 393.1) can lead to a deamidation process, thus lead to the generation of TC 3 (m/z = 393.1). Similarly, the original TC can be transformed directly via the demethylation, deamidation and hydroxylation process. As shown in path II, TC 4 (m/z = 408.2) originated from losing methyl group and amido group of original TC and the deamidation of TC 2. Then TC 7 (m/z = 349.1) was generated via a ketone group oxidation and deamination process. For path III, the TC transform to TC 6 (m/z = 433.1) via the demethylation and ketone group oxidation process and then the TC 5 (m/z = 374.1) was resulted. Noteworthily, the opening ring intermediates, including TC 8 (m/z = 305.1), TC 9 (m/z = 301.1), TC 10 (m/z = 261.1), TC 11 (m/z = 217.1), TC 12 (m/z = 149.1) and TC 13 (m/z = 114.1), were produced along with cleavage of functional groups and the ring-opening reactions. Finally, these opening ring intermediates were oxidized into CO2 and H2O.

According to the experimental results and discussions presented above, the possible photocatalytic mechanism of the BiVO4/Ag@N-CQDs composite is proposed and illustrated in Fig. 13 . Under visible light irradiation, the BiVO4 can directly harvest the light and result in excitation of electrons. As slightly narrowed band-gap of the BiVO4/Ag@N-CQDs composite does not significantly affect the light absorption, the NIR light with low photonic energy is unable to be utilized. Noteworthily, the N-CQDs with up-conversion effect convert the long wavelength light to short wavelength light, which are further utilized by Ag and then enhance the SPR effect of Ag. Thus the BiVO4 can be excited. Unfortunately, the redox ability of CB electrons on BiVO4/Ag@N-CQDs (0.35 eV) is weaker than the potential of O2/O2 − (−0.33 eV), thus the O2 − cannot be produced. The photo-excited electrons on VB of BiVO4 can transfer to CB, which are rapidly trapped by the Ag@N-CQDs core–shell structures. Simultaneously, the hot electrons originated from SPR effect of Ag are pumped into the surface of N-CQDs due to intimate contact between Ag and N-CQDs in the advanced core–shell structures. Besides, the generated hot electrons decay and release heat, thus the photothermal effect also accelerates the separation of photo-excited charges. Finally, the electrons accumulate in the surface of N-CQDs. Thus the electrons on the surface of N-CQDs react with dissolved O2 to generate O2 −. This phenomenon can be explained as the oxygen defects on the surface of N-CQDs directly exposed to the dissolved O2 convert them into •O2 − radical. The h+ remaining on VB of BiVO4/Ag@N-CQDs composite can partly directly react with TC. The other h+ on the VB can react with H2O to produce •OH. Consequently, the enhanced photocatalytic performance stems from the greatly promoted separation of electron-hole pairs and molecular oxygen activation. Simultaneously, the achievement of absorption of long wavelength Vis-NIR light also contributes to the photocatalytic activity.

In summary, the BiVO4/Ag@N-CQDs photocatalyst possesses a sufficient long-wavelength Vis-NIR light absorption, high efficiency of charge separation and strong oxidation ability, thus exhibiting good photocatalytic performance for the degradation of TC under Vis-NIR light. The efficiencies of tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) removal were 80.37% and 65.67% under visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm) for 60 min and NIR light irradiation (λ > 760 nm) for 180 min, respectively. The improved photocatalytic activity of BiVO4/Ag@N-CQDs composite can be ascribed to the synergistic effects as following reasons: (i) the synergy of up-conversion of N-CQDs and SPR effect of Ag in the Ag@N-CQDs core–shell structures enables the efficient Vis-NIR light harvesting; (ii) the SPR effect of Ag in core–shell structures provides hot electrons for the photocatalytic activity; (iii) large number of defects on the surface of N-CQDs extremely promote the molecular oxygen activation in the photocatalytic reaction process. The results not only offer an explanation for the photocatalytic mechanism of BiVO4/Ag@N-CQDs composite, but also provide some insight into synthesis of Vis-NIR responsive photocatalyst. For future practical applications, it is worth looking forward to develop of the non-noble metal Vis-NIR responsive photocatalyst.
